title: Why deletes create tombstones
description: A delete writes a tombstone marker rather than removing data, and reads must skip tombstones until compaction reclaims them.
body: Reference for understanding delete cost. Because Cassandra is a distributed log-structured store, a delete cannot erase data on every replica immediately; it writes a tombstone that masks the old value and survives until gc_grace_seconds passes and compaction removes it. Use when a workload deletes or overwrites rows. Do not treat deletes as free or instantaneous; a read across many tombstones is slow and can hit a failure threshold. Validation signals include tracing showing tombstones scanned per read and that count growing with delete volume. Risk boundary: tombstones must survive at least gc_grace_seconds to prevent data resurrection, so do not shorten that window to hide a tombstone problem; redesign the access pattern instead.
